quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rectilinear motion"?

options.text: Use similar triangles to relate the heights and distances of an object and its shadow; differentiate to find shadow length rate
Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rectilinear motion" is defined as: Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "When is the particle at rest?"?

options.text: A point where the concavity of f changes; requires f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND a sign change in f''
When v(t) = s'(t) = 0; the particle momentarily stops (may or may not change direction)
The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'

quiz_questions.explanation: "When is the particle at rest?" is defined as: When v(t) = s'(t) = 0; the particle momentarily stops (may or may not change direction). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "When does the particle change direction?"?

options.text: When v(t) = 0 AND v(t) changes sign; the particle reverses its direction of travel
F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C
Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t

quiz_questions.explanation: "When does the particle change direction?" is defined as: When v(t) = 0 AND v(t) changes sign; the particle reverses its direction of travel. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Position vs. displacement vs. distance"?

options.text: The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema
Position: s(t); displacement: s(b)-s(a); distance: ∫|v(t)|dt; all three are different quantities
Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1
A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant

quiz_questions.explanation: "Position vs. displacement vs. distance" is defined as: Position: s(t); displacement: s(b)-s(a); distance: ∫|v(t)|dt; all three are different quantities. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Average velocity vs. average speed"?

options.text: f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C
P = K: if the population is perturbed slightly above or below K, it returns to K
Average velocity = displacement/time = [s(b)-s(a)]/(b-a); average speed = distance/time = [∫|v(t)|dt]/(b-a)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Average velocity vs. average speed" is defined as: Average velocity = displacement/time = [s(b)-s(a)]/(b-a); average speed = distance/time = [∫|v(t)|dt]/(b-a). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)"?

options.text: Lorenz curve
Limit does not exist
Interval additivity
Rectilinear motion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rectilinear motion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When v(t) = s'(t) = 0; the particle momentarily stops (may or may not change direction)"?

options.text: MVT with restrictions
Infinite limit
Concavity of F
When is the particle at rest?

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "When is the particle at rest?".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When v(t) = 0 AND v(t) changes sign; the particle reverses its direction of travel"?

options.text: Verification technique
When does the particle change direction?
Fractional exponent derivatives
Conservative forces

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "When does the particle change direction?".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Position: s(t); displacement: s(b)-s(a); distance: ∫|v(t)|dt; all three are different quantities"?

options.text: Domain restrictions
Position vs. displacement vs. distance
Derivative of tan x
Implicit differentiation with time

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Position vs. displacement vs. distance".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Average velocity = displacement/time = [s(b)-s(a)]/(b-a); average speed = distance/time = [∫|v(t)|dt]/(b-a)"?

options.text: Combined technique
Fractional exponent derivatives
Sign chart of f''
Average velocity vs. average speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Average velocity vs. average speed".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Rectilinear Motion is accurate?

options.text: Then solve algebraically for dy/dx.

Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).
These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
Rectilinear Motion analyzes objects moving along a straight line using calculus.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rectilinear Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: The sum, difference, product, quotient, and power laws allow limits to be computed piece by piece.
Linear approximation uses the tangent line to estimate function values near a known point: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).

Given a position function s(t), all motion characteristics are determined: velocity v(t) = s'(t), acceleration a(t) = s''(t), turning points (where v(t) = 0 and changes sign), and intervals of increasing/decreasing position.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rectilinear Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: Particle motion problems require careful attention to signs: positive velocity means moving in the positive direction, and the total distance traveled requires integrating |v(t)|.
The inflection point occurs at P = K/2, where the population is growing fastest.
The key insight is that pressure increases with depth, so different parts of the plate experience different pressures.
After integrating in terms of u, substitute back: u = g(x).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rectilinear Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: An infinite limit lim(x→c) f(x) = ±∞ means f(x) increases or decreases without bound as x approaches c.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.
Graphs also reveal asymptotic behavior and discontinuities.
Rectilinear Motion analyzes objects moving along a straight line using calculus.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rectilinear Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: Given a position function s(t), all motion characteristics are determined: velocity v(t) = s'(t), acceleration a(t) = s''(t), turning points (where v(t) = 0 and changes sign), and intervals of increasing/decreasing position.
After integrating in terms of u, substitute back: u = g(x).
Continuity and Types of Discontinuity classifies discontinuities into three types.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis
Error propagation bounds: if a measuring instrument's response function has bounded derivative, MVT limits the maximum output error from a given input error
Modeling an elevator: s(t) must be continuous and differentiable, with v(t) bounded for safety and a(t) limited for passenger comfort (smooth ride)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rectilinear Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: Population modeling: P(t) = P₀e^(rt), so P'(t) = rP₀e^(rt) = rP(t), showing exponential growth rate is proportional to population size
Compound interest with variable rates: dA/dt for A = P·e^(∫r(t)dt) requires the chain rule combined with the FTC for variable-rate financial modeling
Bacterial growth: E. coli doubles every 20 minutes in ideal conditions; 1 bacterium becomes ~70 billion in 12 hours (2³⁶ ≈ 6.9 × 10¹⁰)
Drag racing analysis: measuring position at checkpoints, computing velocity and acceleration to optimize launch and shifting strategy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rectilinear Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Rectilinear Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: A ball thrown upward returns to its starting height: Rolle's Theorem guarantees it had zero velocity (was momentarily at rest) at some point during its flight
Robotics arm movement: planning smooth one-dimensional trajectories with constraints on velocity and acceleration to prevent mechanical stress
Radioactive decay: dN/dt = -λN separates to dN/N = -λdt, giving N = N₀e^(-λt)—the exponential decay model
Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rectilinear Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Rectilinear Motion?

options.text: Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Modeling an elevator: s(t) must be continuous and differentiable, with v(t) bounded for safety and a(t) limited for passenger comfort (smooth ride)
Ladder sliding down a wall: if the base moves away at 2 ft/s, how fast does the top slide down? Classic Pythagorean related rates problem used in construction safety
Radioactive decay: dN/dt = -λN separates to dN/N = -λdt, giving N = N₀e^(-λt)—the exponential decay model

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rectilinear Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Rectilinear Motion in practice?

options.text: Drag racing analysis: measuring position at checkpoints, computing velocity and acceleration to optimize launch and shifting strategy
Average temperature: f_avg = (1/24)∫₀²⁴ T(t)dt gives the average temperature over a 24-hour period, used for energy consumption estimates
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event
Scientific measurement: using increasingly precise instruments to estimate a true value—smaller measurement intervals approximate the 'limit' of the quantity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rectilinear Motion.
